entries.end_notes: Iranian.

This common West Semitic root is no longer used in normative Aramaic, although it was still in use in the dead-end dialects of Sam'al and Deir Alla in the Old Aramaic period and is the immediate etymon of the presentative particle ˀrw. In Samaritan it is a blatant Hebraism. It may have a remnant in the relatively rare form rhy vb. in Syriac; q.v.
A by-form of rwm, in most texts used only when the connotation is that of haughtiness.

entries.form_section: In Syriac the plural form varies with the meaning (perhaps other than in poetry): "chief, noble one," etc. follows the adjectival, reduplicative patterns with or without final -ān (see s.v. rb adj.), while "teacher" uses r:ab.Ane) ; JBA pl. rby, rbwwt) ; many compounds keep the singular form rb even in the plural of the compound, e.g. rb(ln:) "chief shepherd"; LJLA uses both rbrbyN and rbrbnyN as the plural for "noble". In Gal. forms with doubled /b/ display a vowel shift /a/>/e/, hence rebbī "rabbi", etc. Compare rbwn. The multiplicity of plural forms in Mandaic currently defies analysis!
entries.end_notes: In Jewish texts normally abbreviated r' as a title. In Jewish texts רבן as a title of respect occurs in embedded Hebrew passages, q.v. See also s.v. rbrbn.

entries.form_section: pl. rūḥīn; Syr. and JBAmb also rwx:t) (JBAmb in the sense "direction, side") and rwxn:) , though the latter may well have an "individuating" connotation. In Sam. only ryx by conflation with ryḥ under Arabic influence.
entries.end_notes:
Gender is usually masc. when used of the Holy Spirit (as well as evil spirits!) in later Syr. texts. (On this see now J. W. Childers, "Patristic Citations and Versional Evidence: The Syriac Version(s) of Chrysostom's Homilies on Matthew and the Old Syriac Text," Le Muséon 115[2002]p. 135.) In early texts the holy spirit is femine, however; see, e.g., D.J. Taylor, CSCO 577 (1999) p. xxi; and the complete discussion in S. Brock, Aram 3(1991): 249ff. Also masculine in CPA.
For all the functionality of this word in Syriac scripture see the extensive discussion in IshGen 112f.

The unique G gloss given by LS2-SL "growing in woods" is, as so many of the unique glosses in that work, simply incorrect. In this case it is based on a mistaken reading of the manuscript, which reads correctly ܕܪܘܿܙ‏; see s.v. rwz vb.

entries.form_section: pl.  <syr>r:m) </syr> in the meaning "pool", of which there is an occasional back-formation in the sg. masc. <syr>rm)</syr>.
entries.end_notes:
LS2 assigns the meaning connected with water to the adjective rām "high", thus implicitly assigning examples of the singular form such as 1Kg 22:38 where this word renders MT בְּרֵכָה to a misunderstanding or simply a change of meaning of the Hebrew. We follow instead Payne-Smith's lexicographical analysis on the whole. See form section.
There appears to be little semantic difference between ṭwr and rmh when referring to mountains.

=MP ramag (s S. Shaked, JAOS 1993 78f.); for fem. gender see Bh Gr2 10(25).
In Rabbinic tradition this kind of mule is offspring of a female horse and male ass, and refuses to be bridled and is thus particularly swift. See discussion in Levy.
We assume that using this image for "a multitude" of people has a negative connotation similar to what it would in English.

entries.form_section: Some examples of C perfect may simply be G with prothetic olaf.
entries.end_notes: The Dt example from GnHex reflects a Greek verb (ἐνεθυμέομαι) that everywhere else in Greek means "to think about". However the BDAG does provide a single usage meaning "to regret" based on the Septuagint's rendering of the Hebrew in this passage. But even if that is correct—and we highly doubt it—that does not mean that the verb used in the Syriac rendering of the LXX means "to regret". Note that GLS s.v. ἐνθυμέομαι does not agree with such a gloss.

Etymologically the same Semitic root as ršy #2 but in this meaning almost certainly influenced by Akkadian usages; see AIOA p. 88.
In the sense of "to lend", it is not clear if there is a regular difference between this verb and yzp in any dialect, but if so, it would appear the r$y involves a pledge whereas yzp involves interest.

entries.primary_gloss: C to set aside, to neglect
entries.end_notes: This verb, unique to Targum Proverbs, where it always renders the unusual usage of Hebrew פרע in the sense of "to avoid" or "to remove" or the like, is of totally uncertain origin. Even the correct root form is uncertain. It could be ršš, rwš, or even a derivative of rˁš in a late Babylonian or Palestinian dialect. The Peshitta rendering differs in each case, so it offers no guidance. Nor do any of the possible roots bear any obvious relationship to the normal usages of BH פרע. We suspect in the final analysis that it is an invention of the translator based on the common BH word רָשׁ, "poor person", which is also found used only in this Aramaic text, but the motivation for such a rendering must remain obscure.

entries.end_notes: We take this unusual usage to be a metathesized form of šry conflated with ršy#2. The semantics have been developed due to three BH usages with conflated pronunciation in Samaritan Hebrew, i.e. הוֹאִיל with its two meanings "to allow (?)" and "to begin" along with החל "to begin". Note that this meaning is found only in the J targum of the Torah where such manipulation of forms and meanings based on Hebrew word play is common. Contrariwise, however, the noun רשו "priorness" (see s.v. ryšw) is found only in the late A text. All is very dubious.
